description:
You are acting as a penetration tester who has been keeping track of discovered
    vulnerabilities in a small SQLite database.  
    A legacy database already exists at

        /home/user/security_scans/scan_results_v1.db

    and contains a single table named “vulnerabilities” with the following columns
        id (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY)  
        host (TEXT)  
        port (INTEGER)  
        vuln (TEXT)

    Management now requires you to:
        1.  Create a *new* database called
                /home/user/security_scans/scan_results_v2.db
            whose table “vulnerabilities” **adds one extra column**
                severity (TEXT)  
            The new column must default to the literal string “unknown”.
        2.  Migrate every existing record from the v1 database into the
            new v2 database.  All migrated rows must keep the same id, host, port
            and vuln values; the severity column must contain the default
            value “unknown” for every imported row.
        3.  Produce a plain-text validation log at

                /home/user/security_scans/migration_report.log

            containing exactly three lines in the order shown:

                MIGRATION_STATUS=SUCCESS
                ROWS_IN_V1=<number_of_rows_that_were_in_the_old_db>
                ROWS_IN_V2=<number_of_rows_now_in_the_new_db>

            • There must be no extra spaces.  
            • Each line ends with a single newline character (Unix LF).  
            • Both row counts must be identical.

    IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS
        • Accomplish the *entire* migration (schema creation,
          data copy, and log generation) with a single shell command.  
          You may chain utilities with “&&”, “;”, “|”, subshells, etc.,
          but the automated grader will consider everything typed on one
          command line as one command.  
        • Do not modify or delete the old v1 database.  
        • Do not hard-code row counts; instead, query the live data
          so the counts in the log are accurate.  
        • Only the two files described above should be changed or created.
          Nothing else in the filesystem should be touched.

    The automated test will verify:
        1. /home/user/security_scans/scan_results_v2.db exists.  
        2. It contains a table “vulnerabilities” whose columns, in order, are  
           id, host, port, vuln, severity (where severity defaults to
           “unknown”).  
        3. All rows from v1 are present and identical, with severity = “unknown”.  
        4. /home/user/security_scans/migration_report.log exists and matches
           the exact three-line format shown above; both row counts must be
           greater than zero and equal to each other.

    You have standard command-line tools (sqlite3, awk, sed, bash, etc.)
    at your disposal.  Remember, keep everything to one compound shell
    command and make sure the log file’s contents are exact—otherwise the
    grader will fail the task.
truth:
<!-- Existing legacy database details (pre-populated before task starts) -->
    <initial_db>/home/user/security_scans/scan_results_v1.db</initial_db>
    <initial_table>vulnerabilities</initial_table>
    <initial_schema>CREATE TABLE vulnerabilities (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, host TEXT, port INTEGER, vuln TEXT);</initial_schema>

    <!-- Precise rows present in the legacy database -->
    <initial_rows>
        1|192.168.0.5|22|OpenSSH 7.2p2 outdated
        2|192.168.0.18|445|SMB v1 enabled
        3|10.0.0.7|80|Apache 2.2 directory listing
    </initial_rows>

    <!-- Expected new schema -->
    <new_db>/home/user/security_scans/scan_results_v2.db</new_db>
    <new_schema>CREATE TABLE vulnerabilities (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, host TEXT, port INTEGER, vuln TEXT, severity TEXT DEFAULT 'unknown');</new_schema>
    <new_rows>
        1|192.168.0.5|22|OpenSSH 7.2p2 outdated|unknown
        2|192.168.0.18|445|SMB v1 enabled|unknown
        3|10.0.0.7|80|Apache 2.2 directory listing|unknown
    </new_rows>

    <!-- Expected validation log -->
    <log_file>/home/user/security_scans/migration_report.log</log_file>
    <log_contents>
MIGRATION_STATUS=SUCCESS
ROWS_IN_V1=3
ROWS_IN_V2=3
    </log_contents>
